title: Memory Slips and 'What Did You Just Say?'
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: When you can't remember what you just heard or where you put your keys.

sections.heading: What Memory Problems Look Like Under Stress

content.text: Frontline folks often report memory slips that feel sudden and alarming: forgetting conversations that just happened, losing track mid-sentence, walking into a room and forgetting why, or blanking on familiar names and procedures. This isn't early dementia - it's your brain prioritizing threat detection over memory encoding. Under chronic stress, the hippocampus (memory center) gets starved of resources while the amygdala (threat center) stays lit up.
Common memory issues in frontline work

items: Forgetting conversations minutes after they happen.
Losing track mid-sentence or mid-task.
Walking into rooms and forgetting why you're there.
Blanking on familiar names, procedures, or locations.
Difficulty retaining new information or instructions.
Repeating yourself because you forgot you already said it.

lines: Stress-related memory problems improve with rest and reduced load. Dementia progresses regardless. If memory issues persist despite recovery efforts, get assessed.

content.text: Chronic stress floods your system with cortisol, which shrinks the hippocampus and disrupts memory encoding. When your nervous system is in threat mode, your brain deprioritizes 'non-essential' functions like remembering where you left your phone. It's trying to keep you alive, not organized. Add poor sleep (memory consolidation happens during deep sleep) and you get a brain that can't hold onto new information.

items: Chronic sleep debt: Memory consolidates during sleep. No sleep = no memory storage.
High baseline arousal: Brain stuck in threat mode can't encode non-threat information.
Multitasking under stress: Divided attention prevents encoding.
Substance use: Alcohol and cannabis disrupt memory formation.
Unprocessed trauma: Intrusive memories crowd out present-moment encoding.

lines: Why this works: Reducing arousal and improving sleep restores hippocampal function. External memory aids (lists, alarms, routines) reduce cognitive load and compensate for temporary deficits.

sections.heading: Field Tools: Working Around Memory Deficits

content.text: You can't force your memory to improve overnight, but you can build systems that don't rely on perfect recall. Offload memory tasks to external tools and reduce the number of things you're trying to hold in your head.

items: Write it down immediately: Phone notes, whiteboard, sticky notes. Don't trust your brain to remember.
Single-task: Do one thing at a time. Multitasking under stress guarantees memory failure.
Use alarms and reminders: Set phone alarms for meds, appointments, and tasks. Automate memory.
Repeat back: When someone gives you info, repeat it back out loud. Strengthens encoding.
Put things in the same place always: Keys, wallet, phone. Routines reduce memory load.
Improve sleep for one week: Even small improvements in sleep restore memory function.

lines: Field rule: Don't rely on memory when tools exist. Your phone is smarter than your stressed brain.

content.text: Memory slips feel personal when your partner forgets conversations or commitments, but they're symptoms of overload, not indifference. Repeating yourself doesn't fix a stressed brain - external systems do.

items: Write things down together: Use shared calendars, whiteboards, or text reminders.
Don't take it personally: Forgetting isn't about you. It's about system overload.
Set up routines: Consistent routines reduce memory demands.
Remind gently without criticism: 'Did you see the text about dinner tonight?' not 'You never remember.'
'You're not even listening': Memory failure feels like disinterest. It's not.
Relying on verbal-only communication: If it's important, write it down.
Testing memory: 'Do you remember what I said yesterday?' creates shame, not solutions.

content.text: Memory problems that don't improve with rest, sleep, and reduced stress need assessment. Rule out medical causes (sleep apnea, thyroid issues, head injury) and cognitive conditions.

items: Memory problems persist despite improved sleep and reduced stress.
You're forgetting safety-critical information at work.
Daily function is impaired (missing appointments, losing important items, safety risks).
Memory loss is progressive or gets worse instead of better.
History of head injuries or concussions.

content.text: Use the worksheet to track memory failures and test external systems. Your goal isn't to fix your brain - it's to build systems that don't rely on memory.

lines: 2) Identify the external tool that would have helped (alarm, note, routine).
4) Use it for the rest of the week.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Memory Slips and 'What Did You Just Say?'
worksheets.intro: Use this to track memory failures and build external systems that don't rely on perfect recall.

question_groups.label: What specific memory failures happened this week?
question_groups.hint: Examples: forgot conversation, lost keys, blanked on name, repeated yourself, forgot why you entered a room.

question_groups.label: What external tools could have prevented each failure?
question_groups.hint: Examples: phone alarm, written note, shared calendar, designated spot for keys, routine.

question_groups.label: What two memory-offload systems will you set up this week?
question_groups.hint: Pick systems you can actually use: phone reminders, one routine, one designated spot.

numbered_prompts.hint: What did your body do first?
What exact behavior will you use next time?

worksheets.closing_note: Don't rely on memory. Build systems that work when your brain doesn't.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Memory Slips and 'What Did You Just Say?' - Partner/Team Check-In
worksheets.intro: Use this with a partner to build shared memory systems that reduce frustration.

question_groups.label: What memory failures frustrate you most?
question_groups.hint: Examples: forgotten conversations, missed commitments, repeated questions, lost items.

question_groups.label: What shared systems could reduce memory demands?
question_groups.hint: Examples: shared calendar, text reminders, whiteboard in kitchen, designated spots for essentials.

question_groups.label: What's one memory system you'll both try this week?
question_groups.hint: Example: 'We'll use shared Google Calendar for all appointments and text each other same-day reminders.'

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means pause and reset?
What specific repair happens after a rupture?

worksheets.closing_note: Keep check-ins short, specific, and repeatable.
